name: Guadiana Natural Trail
name_original: Los dos ríos: Río Caya y Río Guadiana - vuelta gravel — circular desde Badajoz
description: Cycle an easy 13.9-mile (22.3 km) route along the Guadiana River, passing Puente Real and the Confluence of the Caya and Guadiana Rivers.
region_intro: No traffic touring cycling routes around the Guadiana traverse a diverse landscape straddling the border between Portugal and Spain. The region is characterized by the majestic Guadiana River, which forms a natural boundary, offering routes along its banks through varied scenery. Cyclists can explore the rugged landscapes and rolling hills of the Guadiana Valley Natural Park, featuring cork oak forests and diverse wildlife. The terrain includes a mix of coastal areas with salt pans, *barrocal* zones, and more inland stretches, providing both flat sections and some challenging ascents. Best no traffic touring cycling routes around the Guadiana The most popular no traffic touring cycling route is From Ayamonte to Isla Cristina – circular via Isla del Moral, a 17.1 miles (27.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 48 minutes to complete, offering coastal views and a cross-border experience. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Guadiana Natural Trail, an easy 13.8 miles (22.3 km) path. This route follows the river, showcasing tranquil natural beauty and low-traffic sections. Local touring cyclists also love the Vía de la Plata and Proserpina Reservoir Loop, a 13.6 miles (21.8 km) trail leading through historic areas and past a reservoir, often completed in about 1 hour 42 minutes. Touring cycling around the Guadiana is defined by the winding Guadiana River, rolling hills, and historic towns. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy riverside paths to more challenging routes through varied terrain. The routes in Guadiana are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 250 reviews. More than 3000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Guadiana's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no-traffic touring cycling routes are available in the Guadiana region?
A: The Guadiana region offers a wide selection of no-traffic touring cycling routes, with over 140 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 13 easy, 70 moderate, and 64 difficult tours.

Q: What is the best season for touring cycling in the Guadiana region?
A: The Guadiana region is generally pleasant for touring cycling outside of the peak summer months. Spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) offer mild temperatures and less rainfall, making them ideal for exploring the diverse landscapes and cultural sites. Summers can be very hot, especially inland.

Q: Are there easy no-traffic touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, the Guadiana region has several easy no-traffic touring routes perfect for beginners or families. These routes typically feature minimal elevation gain and well-maintained surfaces. An excellent example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e986950657/from-ayamonte-to-isla-cristina-circular-via-isla-del-moral" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Ayamonte to Isla Cristina – circular via Isla del Moral&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a gentle ride along the coast.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect on these no-traffic touring routes?
A: The no-traffic touring routes in Guadiana traverse a diverse range of landscapes. You can expect to cycle along the majestic Guadiana River, through the rugged hills and cork oak forests of the Guadiana Valley Natural Park, and past coastal areas with salt pans. The region offers a mix of mountain, &lt;em&gt;barrocal&lt;/em&gt; (transitional zone), and flat riverbank terrain, ensuring varied scenery.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes in the Guadiana region?
A: Yes, many no-traffic touring routes in the Guadiana region are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021532444/from-vegas-bajas-to-the-canal-de-montijo-circular-route-via-talavera-la-real" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Vegas Bajas to the Canal de Montijo — circular route via Talavera la Real&lt;/a&gt; offers a pleasant loop through agricultural landscapes.

Q: What historical sites and attractions can I explore along the no-traffic touring routes?
A: The Guadiana region is rich in history and culture. Many routes pass by charming historic towns and impressive landmarks. You can visit sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/612540" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monsaraz Castle&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/681925" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Marim Castle&lt;/a&gt;, or the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6260983" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monsaraz Historic Village&lt;/a&gt;. Further inland, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4744470" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mértola Castle&lt;/a&gt; offers dramatic views over the river.

Q: Can I find routes that offer a cross-border experience between Portugal and Spain?
A: Absolutely. The Guadiana River forms a natural border, and many routes allow you to experience both Portuguese and Spanish cultures. Towns like Vila Real de Santo António and Ayamonte are common starting points, and you can often find ferry crossings to move between countries, adding a unique element to your tour.

Q: What do other touring cyclists appreciate most about the no-traffic routes in Guadiana?
A: The no-traffic touring routes in Guadiana are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars. Reviewers often praise the tranquil atmosphere, the diverse and captivating landscapes, and the opportunity to explore rich cultural heritage away from busy roads.

Q: Are there any routes that follow the Guadiana River closely?
A: Yes, the Guadiana River is a central feature of the region, and several routes closely follow its banks, offering ever-changing scenery. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021539394/guadiana-natural-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadiana Natural Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, providing a scenic ride along the river's course.

Q: What wildlife might I encounter on the no-traffic touring routes?
A: Cycling through the Guadiana Valley Natural Park and other protected areas offers excellent opportunities for wildlife spotting. You might encounter diverse species such as wild boar, various birds of prey including vultures, and if you're very lucky, the elusive Iberian lynx. The serene rural environment is perfect for observing local fauna.

Q: Are there options for public transport access to these routes?
A: Public transport options vary by location within the Guadiana region. Larger towns like Vila Real de Santo António and Ayamonte often have bus or train connections that can accommodate bicycles, though it's always advisable to check specific operator policies and schedules in advance. For more remote starting points, a car might be necessary.

Q: Where can I park my car when starting a no-traffic touring route?
A: Many towns and villages along the routes, especially those serving as common starting points like Vila Real de Santo António, Castro Marim, or Mértola, offer public parking facilities. It's recommended to check local parking regulations and availability for your chosen starting location beforehand.
tips: It is the fourth bridge over the Guadiana River as it passes through Badajoz. It is called Real because two years before it was the Kings of Spain who laid the first stone at the beginning of its construction.
Wonderful place where the two rivers meet that after a few kilometers flow into the Mediterranean Sea. The place is calm to see how the two rivers come together.

faqs.question: How long does it take to cycle the Guadiana Natural Trail?
faqs.answer: The overall Guadiana Natural Trail is extensive, with sections in Spain (GR 114, over 850 km) and Portugal (GR15, 78 km, or longer touring options up to 368 km). The specific komoot route, a circular gravel return from Badajoz, is approximately 22.3 km and typically takes around 1 hour and 50 minutes to complete by touring bicycle.

faqs.question: Is the Guadiana Natural Trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The difficulty of the Guadiana Natural Trail varies significantly. While some sections, like the 22.3 km circular route from Badajoz, are considered easy with gentle slopes, other parts can be quite challenging with demanding uphill gradients and unpaved segments. Beginners should choose shorter, well-maintained sections, especially those on former railway lines (Vías Verdes).

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse, featuring wide dirt tracks, forest paths, and paved secondary roads with minimal traffic. You'll also encounter sinuous paths, mountainous landscapes, and occasional steep slopes. Coastal areas near the Guadiana River mouth are generally flatter. Some unpaved segments may require dismounting and pushing your bike.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers diverse scenery. You'll consistently follow the Guadiana River and pass notable landmarks such as the Puente Real, Badajoz, the Confluence of the Caya and Guadiana Rivers, and the Guadiana River Footbridge. Further along the broader trail, you can expect old railway lines, olive fields, mountainous regions, wetlands, historical villages, and medieval castles.

faqs.question: What wildlife or plants can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The trail passes through the Guadiana Valley Natural Park, a protected area rich in biodiversity. Keep an eye out for endangered species like the Iberian Lynx, various eagles, and storks. The landscape features oak and cork tree forests, rockroses, dry fields, and Mediterranean scrublands.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Guadiana Natural Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed year-round. However, be mindful of potential rain during winter months and high temperatures in summer. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant cycling conditions with milder weather.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Guadiana Natural Trail?
faqs.answer: For the circular route starting from Badajoz, you can typically find parking within the city or near access points to the trail. Specific parking areas are often available close to major bridges or recreational zones along the river, such as near the Puente Real.

faqs.question: Is the Guadiana Natural Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While specific regulations can vary by section, dogs are generally permitted on the Guadiana Natural Trail. It is always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in protected areas like the Guadiana Valley Natural Park, to protect wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all trail users.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay entrance fees to cycle the trail?
faqs.answer: No, generally you do not need a permit or have to pay entrance fees to cycle the Guadiana Natural Trail. Access to the natural trail and its various sections is typically free and open to the public. However, if you plan to visit specific attractions or natural parks along the way, check their individual entry requirements.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Camino Natural del Guadiana, the Camino Natural del Corredor Cáceres - Badajoz, and the Ecovia 11 (Lisboa - Badajoz).

faqs.question: Is the 22.3 km komoot route a loop trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the specific komoot route provided is a circular gravel return route that starts and ends in Badajoz, making it a convenient loop for touring cyclists.

waypoints.name: Confluence of the Caya and Guadiana Rivers
